title: Synthesis of Urea
description: Wöhler made urea — a molecule from living things — out of inorganic salts, showing that the chemistry of life follows the same rules as the rest of chemistry.

title: Proteins Recognized
description: Mulder identified a common nitrogen-rich substance in living tissue, and Berzelius suggested the name 'protein' — from the Greek for 'of first importance'.

title: Structure of Sugars
description: Fischer worked out the three-dimensional arrangement of glucose and related sugars, laying the groundwork for understanding carbohydrates and stereochemistry in biology.

title: The Peptide Bond
description: Fischer and Hofmeister proposed that proteins are chains of amino acids linked by peptide bonds — the polymer principle behind all protein structure.

title: The Hydrogen Bond
description: Latimer and Rodebush described the hydrogen bond, which explains water's cohesion, high heat capacity, and solvent power — the properties that make it the medium of life.

title: The Alpha Helix
description: Pauling and Corey predicted the α-helix and β-sheet, revealing that a protein's chain folds into regular shapes — the first glimpse of how sequence becomes structure.

title: First Protein Sequenced
description: Sanger determined the complete amino-acid sequence of insulin, proving that each protein has a defined, unique order of building blocks.

title: Protein Folding
description: Anfinsen showed that a denatured protein can refold itself, demonstrating that a protein's amino-acid sequence alone dictates its final three-dimensional shape.

title: Discovery of Cells
description: Peering at cork through a microscope, Hooke saw tiny compartments and named them 'cells' — the first sight of the basic unit of life.

title: Microorganisms
description: With handmade lenses, van Leeuwenhoek observed bacteria and protists — his 'animalcules' — revealing an entire living world invisible to the eye.

title: The Nucleus
description: Brown noticed a consistent dense body inside plant cells and named it the nucleus, later understood to house the cell's genetic material.

title: Cell Theory
description: Schleiden and Schwann concluded that all plants and animals are made of cells — the unifying idea that every living thing is built from the same basic unit.

title: Cells from Cells
description: Virchow's dictum 'omnis cellula e cellula' — every cell comes from a pre-existing cell — completed cell theory and framed growth, healing, and disease.

title: The Lipid Membrane
description: Observing which substances enter cells, Overton deduced that the cell is bounded by a fatty (lipid) layer — the first insight into the membrane's chemistry.

title: The Golgi Apparatus
description: Using a silver stain of his own invention, Golgi revealed the internal membrane network that sorts and ships the cell's products.

title: The Lipid Bilayer
description: Gorter and Grendel measured the lipids in red blood cell membranes and found exactly enough for a double layer — the bilayer at the heart of every membrane.

title: The Lysosome
description: By spinning cells apart, de Duve discovered the lysosome, the membrane-bound sac of enzymes that digests and recycles cellular material.

title: Fluid Mosaic Model
description: Singer and Nicolson pictured the membrane as a fluid sea of lipids studded with drifting proteins — the model of membrane structure students still learn.

title: Endosymbiosis
description: Margulis argued that mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living bacteria engulfed by an ancestral cell — a once-heretical idea now central to how complex cells arose.

title: Aquaporins
description: Agre identified the protein channels that let water rush across membranes, explaining how cells move water far faster than diffusion allows.

title: The First Enzyme
description: Payen and Persoz isolated diastase, which breaks starch into sugar, giving the first clear example of a biological catalyst.

title: Lock-and-Key Model
description: Fischer proposed that an enzyme fits its substrate like a key in a lock, explaining why enzymes act only on specific molecules.

title: Cell-Free Fermentation
description: Buchner showed that juice squeezed from dead yeast still ferments sugar, proving that enzymes — not a mysterious 'life force' — carry out metabolism.

title: Enzymes Are Proteins
description: Sumner crystallized the enzyme urease and demonstrated it was a protein, settling a long debate over what enzymes are made of.

title: Plants Restore Air
description: Priestley found that a plant could 'restore' air made unbreathable by a candle, the first hint that plants release the oxygen animals need.

title: Photosynthesis
description: Ingenhousz showed that plants purify air only in sunlight and only in their green parts, identifying light as the driver of photosynthesis.

title: Water Splits in Photosynthesis
description: Studying photosynthetic bacteria, van Niel deduced that the oxygen plants release comes from splitting water, not carbon dioxide.

title: The Calvin Cycle
description: Using radioactive carbon, Calvin and Benson traced how photosynthesis builds sugar from carbon dioxide — the light-independent reactions.

title: Fermentation Is Life
description: Pasteur showed that fermentation is carried out by living microbes, tying metabolism to organisms and laying the ground for the germ theory of disease.

title: The Krebs Cycle
description: Krebs pieced together the citric acid cycle, the central hub of respiration that harvests energy from food and feeds the cell's power supply.

title: ATP as Energy Currency
description: Lipmann recognized ATP as the universal carrier of chemical energy, the rechargeable molecule that powers nearly everything a cell does.

title: Chemiosmosis
description: Mitchell proposed that cells make ATP by pumping protons across a membrane and letting them flow back — the surprising mechanism behind respiration and photosynthesis.

title: The First Hormone
description: Bayliss and Starling discovered secretin, a chemical messenger released by the gut — the first hormone, and proof that cells signal one another chemically.

title: Mitosis
description: Flemming stained dividing cells and watched threads (chromosomes) split and move apart, describing mitosis — how one cell becomes two identical cells.

title: Second Messengers
description: Sutherland found that hormones act at the cell surface by triggering an internal messenger (cyclic AMP), revealing how a signal outside a cell changes what happens inside.

title: Cell-Cycle Genes
description: Hartwell found yeast mutants stuck at specific points of division, revealing the genes and checkpoints that control the cell cycle.

title: Cyclins
description: Hunt discovered cyclins, proteins that rise and fall to drive the cell through each stage of division — the clock of the cell cycle.

title: Apoptosis
description: Kerr, Wyllie, and Currie named apoptosis — programmed cell death — showing that cells can be instructed to die in an orderly way, essential to development and health.

title: Mendel's Laws
description: Breeding pea plants, Mendel discovered that traits pass to offspring in predictable ratios through discrete 'factors' — the foundation of genetics, ignored for 34 years.

title: Meiosis
description: Van Beneden observed that egg and sperm each carry half the usual number of chromosomes, revealing the reduction division that keeps chromosome number constant across generations.

title: Mendel Rediscovered
description: Three botanists independently rediscovered Mendel's forgotten laws in the same year, finally launching genetics as a science.

title: Chromosome Theory
description: Sutton and Boveri independently linked Mendel's 'factors' to chromosomes, proposing that genes ride on chromosomes that pair and separate in meiosis.

title: The Word 'Genetics'
description: Bateson championed Mendel's ideas and coined 'genetics' for the new science of heredity and variation.

title: Gene, Genotype, Phenotype
description: Johannsen coined 'gene', 'genotype', and 'phenotype', separating an organism's hereditary makeup from its observable traits.

title: Fruit Fly Genetics
description: Morgan's white-eyed fruit fly tied a specific gene to the X chromosome, giving the chromosome theory its first hard proof and making Drosophila the workhorse of genetics.

title: The First Genetic Map
description: Reasoning that genes crossing over less often lie closer together, undergraduate Sturtevant drew the first map of genes along a chromosome.

title: Crossing Over Seen
description: Creighton and McClintock matched a visible chromosome swap to an exchange of traits in maize, showing genetic recombination physically happening.

title: Discovery of DNA
description: Miescher isolated a phosphorus-rich substance from cell nuclei he called 'nuclein' — the molecule we now know as DNA, decades before its role was understood.

title: Bacterial Transformation
description: Griffith found that dead bacteria could pass a trait to live ones, revealing a 'transforming principle' that carries hereditary information between cells.

title: DNA Carries Heredity
description: Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty showed the transforming principle was DNA, not protein — the first strong evidence that DNA is the molecule of heredity.

title: DNA, Not Protein
description: Using radioactively labeled viruses, Hershey and Chase proved that DNA — not protein — enters cells to direct heredity, convincing the last skeptics.

title: Chargaff's Rules
description: Chargaff found that DNA always has equal amounts of A and T, and of G and C — a clue that turned out to reveal how the two strands pair.

title: X-ray of DNA
description: Franklin's X-ray image 'Photo 51' captured DNA's helical shape and dimensions, the crucial data that made the double helix solvable.

title: DNA Double Helix
description: Watson and Crick deduced DNA's double helix of paired bases, immediately suggesting how genes are copied — arguably biology's most famous discovery.

title: Semiconservative Replication
description: Meselson and Stahl proved DNA copies itself by splitting into two strands that each template a new one — confirming the mechanism the double helix implied.

title: DNA Polymerase
description: Kornberg isolated the enzyme that builds new DNA strands, showing that replication is carried out by a specific molecular machine.

title: Central Dogma
description: Crick framed the flow of genetic information — DNA to RNA to protein — the organizing principle for how genes build organisms.

title: Cracking the Genetic Code
description: Nirenberg and Matthaei matched the first RNA codon to an amino acid, beginning to break the code that translates genes into proteins.

title: The lac Operon
description: Jacob and Monod discovered how bacteria switch genes on and off, introducing gene regulation — the idea that cells control which genes are used and when.

title: Recombinant DNA
description: Cohen and Boyer combined DNA from different organisms and put it to work in bacteria, launching genetic engineering and the biotechnology industry.

title: DNA Sequencing
description: Sanger devised a way to read the exact order of bases in DNA, the technique that made reading whole genomes possible.

title: Split Genes & Introns
description: Roberts and Sharp found that genes contain non-coding stretches (introns) removed from RNA, revealing that eukaryotic genes are edited before use.

title: PCR
description: Mullis invented the polymerase chain reaction, which copies a tiny sample of DNA billions of times — the engine of modern genetics, forensics, and diagnostics.

title: RNA Interference
description: Fire and Mello discovered that small RNA molecules can silence genes, revealing a hidden layer of gene regulation and a powerful research tool.

title: CRISPR
description: Doudna and Charpentier turned a bacterial defense system into CRISPR-Cas9, a programmable tool that edits DNA with unprecedented ease.

title: Classifying Life
description: Linnaeus built a system of ranked groups and two-part Latin names for every species, giving biology a shared language for the diversity of life.

title: Extinction Is Real
description: Comparing fossils to living animals, Cuvier proved that some species had vanished forever, establishing extinction as a fact of the history of life.

title: Struggle for Existence
description: Malthus argued that populations grow faster than their food supply, creating a struggle to survive — the insight that later sparked Darwin's theory.

title: Lamarck's Evolution
description: Lamarck offered the first full theory that species change over time. His mechanism — inheriting traits acquired in life — was wrong, but the idea of evolution took hold.

title: Deep Time
description: Lyell argued that slow, everyday processes shaped Earth over immense spans of time — the deep time that gave evolution room to occur.

title: Natural Selection
description: Darwin and Wallace independently proposed natural selection: heritable variations that aid survival become more common over generations — the unifying theory of biology.

title: Archaeopteryx
description: The fossil of Archaeopteryx, part dinosaur and part bird, appeared two years after Darwin's book — a striking transitional form linking major groups.

title: Hardy–Weinberg Equilibrium
description: Hardy and Weinberg showed how allele frequencies stay constant without evolution, giving biologists a baseline to detect when a population is evolving.

title: Primordial Soup
description: Oparin and Haldane proposed that life's building blocks formed spontaneously in the chemistry of the early Earth — the primordial soup hypothesis.

title: The Modern Synthesis
description: Dobzhansky and others merged Darwin's natural selection with Mendelian genetics, explaining evolution as changes in gene frequencies within populations.

title: The Biological Species
description: Mayr defined a species as a group that interbreeds, and explained how geographic isolation splits one species into two.

title: Origin of Life
description: Miller and Urey sparked a flask of simple gases and produced amino acids, showing that life's building blocks can arise from non-living chemistry.

title: Peppered Moths
description: Kettlewell documented dark moths outnumbering light ones in soot-stained industrial England, a textbook case of natural selection observed in the wild.

title: Three Domains of Life
description: Comparing RNA sequences, Woese uncovered the archaea and redrew the tree of life into three great domains, reshaping how we classify all organisms.

title: Darwin's Finches Evolve
description: On a Galápagos island, the Grants measured finch beaks shifting within a few years of drought — evolution by natural selection caught in the act.

title: Biogeography
description: Humboldt mapped how plants change with climate and altitude, founding the study of where life lives and why — the roots of ecology.

title: Logistic Growth
description: Verhulst modeled how population growth slows as resources run short, producing the S-shaped logistic curve and the idea of carrying capacity.

title: The Word 'Ecology'
description: Haeckel coined 'ecology' for the study of organisms in relation to their environment, naming a field that Darwin's ideas had made necessary.

title: Nitrogen Fixation
description: Beijerinck isolated the root-nodule bacteria that convert air's nitrogen into a form plants can use, revealing a key link in the nutrient cycles of ecosystems.

title: Predator–Prey Cycles
description: Lotka and Volterra wrote equations showing how predator and prey populations rise and fall in linked cycles, bringing mathematics into ecology.

title: Food Chains & Niches
description: Elton described food chains, food webs, and the ecological niche, giving ecology the vocabulary for how energy and roles are organized in a community.

title: Competitive Exclusion
description: Gause showed that two species competing for the exact same resource cannot coexist indefinitely — one always wins, shaping how communities assemble.

title: The Ecosystem
description: Tansley coined 'ecosystem' to capture organisms and their physical environment as a single interacting system of matter and energy.

title: Energy Flow
description: Lindeman traced energy passing up through trophic levels and dwindling at each step, turning the ecosystem into something measurable — the '10% rule' of energy transfer.

title: Silent Spring
description: Carson documented how pesticides ripple through food webs, awakening public awareness of ecology and launching the modern environmental movement.

title: Keystone Species
description: By removing a single starfish from a tidal community, Paine showed that some species hold an entire ecosystem together — the keystone species concept.

title: Island Biogeography
description: MacArthur and Wilson explained how island size and isolation set the number of species, a theory now central to conservation and reserve design.
